function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing defines MAU and daily posts separately. What specific caveat accompanies each metric, and what do the caveats imply regarding the reliability and interpretability of AI engagement metrics?

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me analyze this question. It's asking about:

1. How the filing defines MAU (Monthly Active Users) and daily posts
2. What specific caveats accompany each metric
3. What these caveats imply about the reliability and interpretability of AI engagement metrics

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing and then searching for the relevant sections about MAU and daily posts definitions and caveats.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me retrieve information about MAU and daily posts definitions, and the caveats associated with each.

arguments.prompt: What is the definition of MAU (Monthly Active Users) and what specific caveats, limitations, or qualifications accompany this metric? Search in {{spacex_s1}}.
What is the definition of "daily posts" and what specific caveats, limitations, or qualifications accompany this metric? Search in {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
The provided text defines MAU (Monthly Active Users) and details several caveats, limitations, and qualifications. The definition and methodology are described in the preamble’s glossary section:

> “MAU” (or monthly active users) refers to the total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement (“active users”). In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU. Furthermore, only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included. While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period.

Specific caveats, limitations, and qualifications that accompany this metric include:

1. **Estimate of unique users** – The MAU figure is described as an “estimate” and “a reasonable approximation” rather than an exact count, because the deduplication methodology “may not fully capture all instances of duplication.”
2. **Registration requirement** – Only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included; unregistered or logged-out interactions are excluded.
3. **Deduplication approach** – The combined MAU across X and Grok attempts to avoid double-counting by identifying users based on sign-in traffic, but the process is acknowledged as imperfect.
4. **Management’s focus is not on MAU growth** – A later section of the prospectus emphasizes that “while MAUs provide an estimated measure of the size and engagement of our user base, we are focused on revenue and operating margin, and manage our business with the objective of driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels.” This signals that MAU is not the primary operational metric and may not reflect business priorities.
5. **Subset tracking** – The company separately tracks the subset of MAUs that used Grok’s AI features based on server request sources, which means the headline MAU number merges users of the social platform and the AI tool, potentially obscuring the level of AI-specific engagement.

Thus, MAU is an estimated, combined, deduplicated count of registered users who had at least one interaction with X or Grok in a rolling 30-day window, subject to measurement limitations and not treated as the principal indicator of business performance.

The definition of "daily posts" and its accompanying qualification are explicitly provided in the preamble's glossary:

**Definition:**
> “daily posts” on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. 

**Caveat / Qualification:**
> This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI.

This caveat indicates that the daily posts metric is not limited to organic human content; it can encompass automated or AI-driven activity, which investors should consider when evaluating the engagement data.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have the definitions and caveats for both MAU and daily posts. Let me also retrieve the more detailed discussion from the filing, particularly any additional context about these caveats and their implications.

arguments.prompt: What additional discussion exists in the filing about AI-generated content, AI-managed accounts, bots, or the reliability of engagement metrics? Look for any disclosure about how AI may affect MAU, daily posts, or other user engagement metrics in {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
The prospectus summary’s disclosure about engagement metrics includes a clear acknowledgment that **AI-generated content and AI-managed accounts may be included in the daily posts metric**, though it does not provide comparable disclosure for MAU or the reliability of those measures. The relevant language appears in the definition of “daily posts” within the chunk that outlines key terms:

> “ **‘daily posts’ on X and Grok** refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. **This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI.** ”  
> (From the fragment beginning with *“to deliver consumer and enterprise applications…”*)

This disclosure indicates that the daily posts metric is not limited to human-generated content; it can be affected by AI-synthesized material and by accounts controlled by AI (which would encompass bots). No similar carve-out or warning is provided for the MAU figure in any of the chunks you supplied. The MAU discussion only states that monthly active users “provide an estimated measure of the size and engagement of our user base” and emphasizes that management prioritizes revenue and operating margin over MAU growth. The filing does not explicitly discuss the reliability of engagement metrics in connection with AI influence beyond this one note in the definition of daily posts.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me also check if there's any more context about the implications of these caveats - maybe in the risk factors section or elsewhere in the filing.

arguments.prompt: Search the risk factors and any other sections of {{spacex_s1}} for any discussion of AI-generated content, bots, fake accounts, automated accounts, or how the presence of AI-generated content or AI-managed accounts could affect the reliability or interpretability of user metrics or engagement metrics.

results.content: No discussion of AI-generated content, bots, fake accounts, automated accounts, or their impact on the reliability or interpretability of user metrics or engagement metrics was found in the provided text. The excerpts focus on other AI risks (output quality, hallucinations, regulatory actions, misuse, etc.) but do not address these specific topics.

steps.reasoning_content: Let me also get the exact language from the filing for both definitions with full context.

arguments.prompt: Retrieve the complete glossary or key terms section of {{spacex_s1}} that defines MAU, daily posts, and any other user engagement or platform metrics. I want to see the exact text of all definitions and caveats.

results.content:
Here are the exact glossary definitions for the key user-engagement and platform metrics appearing in the Preamble’s terms section, along with all caveats and methodological notes. The text is stitched together from the [SECTION: PREAMBLE] fragments you provided.

---

**MAU (Monthly Active Users)**
> “MAU” (or monthly active users) refers to the total number of users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement (“active users”). In presenting combined MAUs across the two platforms, we seek to identify and account for users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic so that such users are not double-counted when measuring MAU. Furthermore, only users who have registered for an X or Grok account are included. While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period. We track the subset of users who used Grok’s AI features and those who have not based on the source of their server requests.

**daily posts (on X and Grok)**
> “daily posts” on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok. This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI.

**supported accounts**
> “supported accounts” refers to, when used in the context of our X platform and Grok, a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok. The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active.

---

If you also need the bridging metric between space capacity and digital engagement (the earlier “mass to orbit” term), let me know and I’ll add that definition.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have a comprehensive picture of the definitions and caveats. Let me also look for any additional context about the "supported accounts" metric and how it relates to the other metrics. The key finding here is that "supported accounts" may include fake, spam, or bot accounts.

Now let me synthesize my answer:

**MAU Caveat:**
- MAU is an estimate, not a precise count. The deduplication methodology "may not fully capture all instances of duplication."
- Only registered users are included (excludes unregistered/logged-out interactions).
- Management explicitly states they are not focused on MAU growth; they prioritize revenue and operating margin.
- The MAU combines X platform users and Grok AI users, which may obscure AI-specific engagement.

**Daily Posts Caveat:**
- "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

**What these caveats imply about reliability and interpretability:**

1. MAU is inherently an estimate subject to measurement error (duplication), which means the reported figure could overstate actual unique users.

2. The daily posts metric conflates human-generated content with AI-generated content and activity from AI-managed accounts. This means daily posts cannot be used to measure genuine human engagement on the platform — the metric could be inflated by automated activity.

3. The "supported accounts" definition further reinforces this concern: it explicitly includes "fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active." This suggests the platform has known issues with non-human accounts.

4. Together, these caveats suggest that both the user base size (MAU) and the activity level (daily posts) could be artificially inflated by AI/bot activity, making it difficult for investors to assess genuine human engagement.

5. The lack of any quantification of the AI-generated or AI-managed component of daily posts means investors cannot separate human from non-human activity.

6. Management's explicit disavowal of MAU as a primary business metric further undermines its usefulness as an investment indicator.

Let me now submit the final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Definitions and Caveats

### MAU (Monthly Active Users)

**Definition (from the S-1 glossary):**
"MAU" refers to the total number of registered users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. The company attempts to deduplicate users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic.

**Caveat:**
> "While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period."

Additional qualifications:
- Only registered users are included; unregistered or logged-out interactions are excluded.
- Management explicitly states: "while MAUs provide an estimated measure of the size and engagement of our user base, we are focused on revenue and operating margin, and manage our business with the objective of driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels."
- The MAU figure merges X social-platform users with Grok AI users; AI-specific engagement is tracked only as a subset via server request sources.

---

### Daily Posts

**Definition (from the S-1 glossary):**
"Daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok.

**Caveat:**
> "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

---

### Related: "Supported Accounts"

The filing also defines "supported accounts" as "a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok," and explicitly notes: "The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active."

---

## Implications for Reliability and Interpretability of AI Engagement Metrics

The caveats accompanying MAU and daily posts carry significant implications:

1. **MAU is an estimate, not a precise count.** The acknowledged inability to "fully capture all instances of duplication" means reported MAU may overstate the true number of unique users. Because MAU blends users of a social-media platform (X) with users of an AI assistant (Grok), an investor cannot determine what share of "engagement" reflects AI adoption versus traditional social-media activity.

2. **Daily posts cannot be interpreted as a measure of human engagement.** The explicit warning that daily posts "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" means the metric conflates human activity with machine-generated content. A rise in daily posts could reflect growing human usage, increased AI-generated output, expanded bot activity, or any combination — and the filing provides no mechanism to disaggregate these components.

3. **The "supported accounts" definition compounds the concern.** By acknowledging that the total account base may include "fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active," the filing signals that the underlying population from which MAU and daily posts are drawn is itself contaminated by non-human or inauthentic accounts.

4. **No quantification is provided.** The filing does not estimate what proportion of MAU or daily posts is attributable to AI, bots, or fake accounts. This makes it impossible for an investor to assess materiality or trend the "clean" human-engagement figures over time.

5. **Management disclaims MAU as an operating priority.** The explicit statement that management does not manage the business with the objective of growing or maintaining MAU suggests that MAU trends may not be predictive of revenue or financial performance — further diminishing its usefulness as an investment metric.

In sum, the caveats reveal that both MAU and daily posts are **composite metrics that blend human and non-human activity across two distinct platforms, are subject to known measurement error, and are not treated by management as primary business indicators.** Investors relying on these metrics to assess platform health, user growth, or AI-adoption trends face a fundamental interpretability problem: the filing does not provide the data needed to isolate genuine human engagement from automated or inauthentic activity.

results.content:
## Definitions and Caveats

### MAU (Monthly Active Users)

**Definition (from the S-1 glossary):**
"MAU" refers to the total number of registered users who have interacted with Grok or X through web browsers or mobile applications at least once during the 30-day period ending on the date of measurement. The company attempts to deduplicate users who access both Grok and X based on sign-in traffic.

**Caveat:**
> "While we believe our methodologies provide a reasonable approximation of MAU based on the number of unique users, they may not fully capture all instances of duplication, and our reported MAU should be viewed as an estimate of unique users across our Grok and X platforms for the applicable period."

Additional qualifications:
- Only registered users are included; unregistered or logged-out interactions are excluded.
- Management explicitly states: "while MAUs provide an estimated measure of the size and engagement of our user base, we are focused on revenue and operating margin, and manage our business with the objective of driving sustainable revenue growth and profitability rather than with the primary objective of growing or maintaining MAU levels."
- The MAU figure merges X social-platform users with Grok AI users; AI-specific engagement is tracked only as a subset via server request sources.

---

### Daily Posts

**Definition (from the S-1 glossary):**
"Daily posts" on X and Grok refers to the aggregate volume of original posts, replies, reposts, quotes, and media shared daily by users on the X platform, and the real-time interactions, analysis, and generative capabilities provided to a user by Grok.

**Caveat:**
> "This may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI."

---

### Related: "Supported Accounts"

The filing also defines "supported accounts" as "a human, bot or similar account that logged into the X platform or Grok," and explicitly notes: "The total number of supported accounts may include fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active."

---

## Implications for Reliability and Interpretability of AI Engagement Metrics

The caveats accompanying MAU and daily posts carry significant implications:

1. **MAU is an estimate, not a precise count.** The acknowledged inability to "fully capture all instances of duplication" means reported MAU may overstate the true number of unique users. Because MAU blends users of a social-media platform (X) with users of an AI assistant (Grok), an investor cannot determine what share of "engagement" reflects AI adoption versus traditional social-media activity.

2. **Daily posts cannot be interpreted as a measure of human engagement.** The explicit warning that daily posts "may include posts generated by AI or accounts managed by AI" means the metric conflates human activity with machine-generated content. A rise in daily posts could reflect growing human usage, increased AI-generated output, expanded bot activity, or any combination — and the filing provides no mechanism to disaggregate these components.

3. **The "supported accounts" definition compounds the concern.** By acknowledging that the total account base may include "fake, spam or bot accounts if they are active," the filing signals that the underlying population from which MAU and daily posts are drawn is itself contaminated by non-human or inauthentic accounts.

4. **No quantification is provided.** The filing does not estimate what proportion of MAU or daily posts is attributable to AI, bots, or fake accounts. This makes it impossible for an investor to assess materiality or trend the "clean" human-engagement figures over time.

5. **Management disclaims MAU as an operating priority.** The explicit statement that management does not manage the business with the objective of growing or maintaining MAU suggests that MAU trends may not be predictive of revenue or financial performance — further diminishing its usefulness as an investment metric.

In sum, the caveats reveal that both MAU and daily posts are **composite metrics that blend human and non-human activity across two distinct platforms, are subject to known measurement error, and are not treated by management as primary business indicators.** Investors relying on these metrics to assess platform health, user growth, or AI-adoption trends face a fundamental interpretability problem: the filing does not provide the data needed to isolate genuine human engagement from automated or inauthentic activity.
